flags.rewound_from_stage0_5_pivot: Case 6b + regression pattern (a): both reviewers reject at the conjecture level — Conjecture 1 is refuted by the note's own three-block finite-support counterexample (row-span failure coexists with Q_DW(P)={0}), Conjecture 2's flagship d_infty-open global monotone-selection claim is explicitly not proved (only a finite-dimensional witness + rank-wise determinant stability), and Theorem 1 collapses to a definition-body restatement of Q_I(P). The kernel is unsalvageable as-conjectured; re-deriving on the same .tex would burn budget. Per-boundary heuristic overridden because the defect is at the proposal layer, not a fixable derivation step.

proposed_from.topic: Distributional synthetic control: sharp identification of the counterfactual outcome distribution for a treated unit under a quantile-rank-invariant interactive factor structure with finitely many donor units
proposed_from.novelty_justification: {"repo_axis":"The closest in-catalogue sources are the downgraded eid_dist_synth_control and pid_synth_control_factor_misspec banked proposals. Those prior nodes concerned row-span/monotone-envelope kernels or scalar Chebyshev synthetic-control intervals. This v3 proposal instead targets the marginal-versus-copula frontier after marginal distributional-SC identification and adds operational diagnostics W(d) and D_Cstar(d), so it is not a relabelling of the banked scalar or row-span proposals.","published_axis":"The closest published papers are Gunsilius2023DiSCo and Chen2020DistributionalSC for marginal distributional synthetic control; FanPark2010SharpBounds and FirpoRidder2019Functionals for sharp DTE/functionals from marginals; Callaway2021BoundsDTEPanel and CallawayLi2019QTEDID for panel dependence restrictions. The epsilon-gap is the DiSCo-specific diagnose-and-correct iff frontier: when a fitted marginal counterfactual curve justifies treatment-effect reporting exactly through W(d)=0 or an explicit copula restriction, and otherwise must be replaced by the LP/Frechet-Makarov envelope."}
seed_list: Prior work Gunsilius2023DiSCo establishes a unique weighted donor quantile function; our seed conjectures that optimizer uniqueness is not causal point identification, because finite-donor rank-factor observational equivalence can preserve every observed quantile panel while moving the treated post quantile curve.

Prior work Bai2009IFE establishes mean interactive fixed effects; our seed conjectures that mean factor span can be distributionally invisible, because rank-specific factor loadings can agree in mean and pre-period quantiles while separating post-period tail quantiles.

Prior work Masini2025DCA estimates a full conditional quantile function for one or few treated units; our seed conjectures that identical high-dimensional pre-period CQF predictions can imply different causal counterfactual quantile laws, because prediction sparsity need not identify the treated unit's latent rank loading.

Prior work FermanPinto2021ImperfectFit shows imperfect mean pre-fit creates SC bias; our seed conjectures that distributional pre-fit error produces asymmetric sharp CDF envelopes, because signed quantile residual directions map differently into lower-tail and upper-tail post-period endpoints.

Prior work Chen2020DistributionalSC identifies distributional effects by matching pre-intervention quantile residuals; our seed conjectures that exact residual matching can still fail to point-identify the post counterfactual distribution, because residual orthogonality need not pin the post-period rank-factor functional.

Prior work DoudchenkoImbens2016Balancing unifies balancing, regression, DID, and synthetic-control weights; our seed conjectures that pre-fit-equivalent convex and signed weights need not be distributionally identification-equivalent, because signed rank leverage changes the sharp quantile set even when mean fit is unchanged.

Prior work AtheyBayatiDoudchenkoImbensKhosravi2021MC connects low-rank matrix completion and SC for mean counterfactuals; our seed conjectures that low-rank mean completion can coexist with non-singleton distributional counterfactual sets, because quantile-specific loadings create post-period null directions absent from the mean matrix.

Prior work AtheyImbens2006CIC identifies counterfactual distributions under monotone rank structure; our seed conjectures that donor-factor rank invariance identifies only the marginal untreated counterfactual distribution, because the treated-outcome copula is not fixed by donor quantile synthesis.

Prior work CallawayLi2019QTEDID identifies QTT using distributional DID plus dependence restrictions; our seed conjectures that distributional synthetic control inherits the same marginal-versus-treatment-effect separation, because rank-invariant donor synthesis alone does not couple Y(1) and Y(0).

Prior work ChenFeng2023GroupHeterogeneousCICDSC argues valid DSC controls may come from different periods with comparable group heterogeneity; our seed conjectures that cross-period donor cells can improve quantile fit while breaking causal interpretation, because time-varying latent quantile factors can mimic heterogeneity without preserving the untreated law.

Prior work Kline2020BoundsDTEPanel bounds distributional treatment-effect parameters using panel dependence restrictions; our seed conjectures that a point-identified DSC marginal counterfactual distribution still leaves a sharp copula-bound problem for treatment-effect distributions, because the joint law of treated and untreated potential outcomes is not identified.

seed_details.one_line: Prior work Gunsilius2023DiSCo establishes a unique weighted donor quantile function; our seed conjectures that optimizer uniqueness is not causal point identification, because finite-donor rank-factor observational equivalence can preserve every observed quantile panel while moving the treated post quantile curve.

anchor_paper.author: Florian F. Gunsilius
anchor_paper.claim: Distributional synthetic controls weight donor quantile functions to form a unique counterfactual quantile function, but the identification argument is not a finite-donor interactive quantile-factor boundary.

seed_details.anti_pattern_check: Boundary/regime-shift pattern; not blocked because the seed predicts nonidentification despite optimizer uniqueness, not a routine assumption weakening.
seed_details.referee_objection: This is immediate from Gunsilius2023DiSCo because the DiSCo quantile function is already unique.
seed_details.rebuttal: Optimizer uniqueness does not characterize the finite-donor latent rank-factor observational equivalence class or prove causal point identification.

seed_details.one_line: Prior work Bai2009IFE establishes mean interactive fixed effects; our seed conjectures that mean factor span can be distributionally invisible, because rank-specific factor loadings can agree in mean and pre-period quantiles while separating post-period tail quantiles.

anchor_paper.author: Jushan Bai
anchor_paper.claim: Interactive fixed effects provide a mean-panel factor model and large-panel estimator, not a sharp finite-donor statement for rank-invariant outcome distributions.

seed_details.anti_pattern_check: Target variation pattern; not blocked because the new target exposes a hidden identification gap between mean factor fit and quantile-factor fit.
seed_details.referee_objection: This is immediate from Bai2009IFE because factor nonidentification is a standard rotation issue.
seed_details.rebuttal: The proposed gap is not factor rotation but post-period distributional nonidentification for a treated unit under fixed finite donors.

seed_details.one_line: Prior work Masini2025DCA estimates a full conditional quantile function for one or few treated units; our seed conjectures that identical high-dimensional pre-period CQF predictions can imply different causal counterfactual quantile laws, because prediction sparsity need not identify the treated unit's latent rank loading.

anchor_paper.author: Ricardo Masini
anchor_paper.claim: Distributional counterfactual analysis estimates a full conditional quantile function for one or few treated units in high-dimensional panels, but gives estimation bounds rather than a finite-donor sharp identification frontier.

seed_details.anti_pattern_check: Estimator-versus-identification pattern; not blocked because it separates predictive CQF equality from causal sharp identification.
seed_details.referee_objection: This is immediate from high-dimensional quantile-regression nonuniqueness because sparse predictors are not unique.
seed_details.rebuttal: The proposal asks for the causal finite-donor identified set, not a computational nonuniqueness fact about penalized regression.

seed_details.one_line: Prior work FermanPinto2021ImperfectFit shows imperfect mean pre-fit creates SC bias; our seed conjectures that distributional pre-fit error produces asymmetric sharp CDF envelopes, because signed quantile residual directions map differently into lower-tail and upper-tail post-period endpoints.

anchor_paper.author: Bruno Ferman and Cristine Pinto
anchor_paper.claim: Imperfect pre-treatment fit is a recognized synthetic-control identification and bias problem, but the distributional sharp-set analogue is not developed there.

seed_details.anti_pattern_check: Imperfect-fit extension pattern; not blocked because the proposed phenomenon is asymmetric endpoint geometry for the full CDF envelope, not a scalar RMSPE generalization.
seed_details.referee_objection: This is immediate from FermanPinto2021ImperfectFit because imperfect fit already causes bias.
seed_details.rebuttal: Mean bias does not imply the sharp distributional endpoint map or its tail-asymmetric geometry.

seed_details.one_line: Prior work Chen2020DistributionalSC identifies distributional effects by matching pre-intervention quantile residuals; our seed conjectures that exact residual matching can still fail to point-identify the post counterfactual distribution, because residual orthogonality need not pin the post-period rank-factor functional.

anchor_paper.author: Yi-Ting Chen
anchor_paper.claim: Distributional SCM allows distributional effects and poor matching through pre-intervention quantile residuals, leaving open a finite-donor sharp identified-set characterization.

seed_details.anti_pattern_check: Counterexample/boundary pattern; not blocked because it targets a named residual-matching DSC workflow and promotes the obstruction to a finite-donor class.
seed_details.referee_objection: This is immediate from Chen2020DistributionalSC because residual matching already controls poor matching.
seed_details.rebuttal: Residual orthogonality is not a necessary-and-sufficient condition for post-period quantile-factor point identification.

seed_details.one_line: Prior work DoudchenkoImbens2016Balancing unifies balancing, regression, DID, and synthetic-control weights; our seed conjectures that pre-fit-equivalent convex and signed weights need not be distributionally identification-equivalent, because signed rank leverage changes the sharp quantile set even when mean fit is unchanged.

anchor_paper.author: Nikolay Doudchenko and Guido W. Imbens
anchor_paper.claim: Balancing, regression, difference-in-differences, and synthetic-control weights can be viewed in one framework, but this does not give distributional finite-donor sharpness equivalence.

seed_details.anti_pattern_check: Estimator-lattice pattern; not blocked because it predicts strict distributional sharp-set separation across named weight classes.
seed_details.referee_objection: This is immediate from DoudchenkoImbens2016Balancing because signed and convex weights are already different restrictions.
seed_details.rebuttal: Different restrictions alone do not establish whether their induced full counterfactual distribution sets coincide or strictly separate.

seed_details.one_line: Prior work AtheyBayatiDoudchenkoImbensKhosravi2021MC connects low-rank matrix completion and SC for mean counterfactuals; our seed conjectures that low-rank mean completion can coexist with non-singleton distributional counterfactual sets, because quantile-specific loadings create post-period null directions absent from the mean matrix.

anchor_paper.author: Susan Athey, Mohsen Bayati, Nikolay Doudchenko, Guido Imbens and Khashayar Khosravi
anchor_paper.venue: Journal of the American Statistical Association
anchor_paper.claim: Matrix completion connects low-rank factor prediction and synthetic control for mean counterfactuals, suggesting an estimator lattice whose distributional analogue is not settled.

seed_details.anti_pattern_check: Target-variation pattern; not blocked because the distributional null direction is absent from the named mean MC result.
seed_details.referee_objection: This is immediate from matrix-completion theory because mean completion does not identify distributions.
seed_details.rebuttal: The nontrivial question is the sharp finite-donor quantile-factor boundary, not the general fact that means are weaker than distributions.

seed_details.one_line: Prior work AtheyImbens2006CIC identifies counterfactual distributions under monotone rank structure; our seed conjectures that donor-factor rank invariance identifies only the marginal untreated counterfactual distribution, because the treated-outcome copula is not fixed by donor quantile synthesis.

anchor_paper.author: Susan Athey and Guido W. Imbens
anchor_paper.claim: Changes-in-changes identifies a counterfactual distribution under a monotone structural model, but relies on strong rank structure rather than donor-factor synthesis.

seed_details.anti_pattern_check: Marginal-versus-joint target pattern; not blocked because it separates marginal DSC identification from treatment-effect distribution identification.
seed_details.referee_objection: This is immediate from CIC because rank invariance already handles the rank coupling.
seed_details.rebuttal: CIC monotonicity is not the same as donor-factor synthesis and does not by itself couple post-treatment treated outcomes with untreated counterfactuals.

seed_details.one_line: Prior work CallawayLi2019QTEDID identifies QTT using distributional DID plus dependence restrictions; our seed conjectures that distributional synthetic control inherits the same marginal-versus-treatment-effect separation, because rank-invariant donor synthesis alone does not couple Y(1) and Y(0).

anchor_paper.author: Brantly Callaway and Tong Li
anchor_paper.claim: Quantile treatment effects on the treated in DiD require distributional DiD plus dependence restrictions between outcome changes and initial untreated outcomes.

seed_details.anti_pattern_check: Target variation pattern; not blocked because switching from marginal CDF to QTT reveals a hidden copula identification gap.
seed_details.referee_objection: This is immediate from CallawayLi2019QTEDID because QTT always needs dependence restrictions.
seed_details.rebuttal: The finite-donor DSC taxonomy of which rank-factor assumptions identify only marginals versus treatment-effect distributions is not stated there.

seed_details.one_line: Prior work ChenFeng2023GroupHeterogeneousCICDSC argues valid DSC controls may come from different periods with comparable group heterogeneity; our seed conjectures that cross-period donor cells can improve quantile fit while breaking causal interpretation, because time-varying latent quantile factors can mimic heterogeneity without preserving the untreated law.

anchor_paper.author: Songnian Chen and Junlong Feng
anchor_paper.claim: Group-heterogeneous distributional synthetic controls argue that valid controls may come from periods with comparable group heterogeneity rather than the same period.

seed_details.anti_pattern_check: Donor-period selection pattern; not blocked because it predicts a fit-validity separation for named cross-period DSC controls.
seed_details.referee_objection: This is immediate from ChenFeng2023GroupHeterogeneousCICDSC because they already impose comparable heterogeneity.
seed_details.rebuttal: Comparable heterogeneity is not a sharp finite-donor condition for causal validity under time-varying latent quantile factors.

seed_details.one_line: Prior work Kline2020BoundsDTEPanel bounds distributional treatment-effect parameters using panel dependence restrictions; our seed conjectures that a point-identified DSC marginal counterfactual distribution still leaves a sharp copula-bound problem for treatment-effect distributions, because the joint law of treated and untreated potential outcomes is not identified.

anchor_paper.author: Patrick Kline
anchor_paper.claim: Distributional treatment-effect parameters depend on the joint distribution of potential outcomes, which is not identified by standard assumptions alone.

seed_details.anti_pattern_check: Marginal-versus-joint target pattern; not blocked because it maps the published DTE bounds problem into finite-donor DSC assumptions.
seed_details.referee_objection: This is immediate from Kline2020BoundsDTEPanel because marginal distributions never identify individual treatment-effect distributions.
seed_details.rebuttal: The open contribution is the DSC-specific classification of which rank-invariance restrictions identify the marginal only and which add copula content.

proposed_from.literature_map: Stage -1.1 literature map reused: Abadie and Gardeazabal (2003) and Abadie, Diamond, and Hainmueller (2010) anchor aggregate mean synthetic control. Bai (2009), Xu (2017), and Athey et al. (2021) connect synthetic control, interactive fixed effects, and low-rank matrix completion for mean outcomes. Athey and Imbens (2006), Fan and Yu (2012), Callaway and Li (2019), and Kline (2020) show that counterfactual distributions and treatment-effect distributions require rank, dependence, or copula restrictions. Chernozhukov, Fernandez-Val, and Melly (2013) provide general counterfactual distribution tools. Chen (2020), Gunsilius (2023), Ferman and Pinto (2021), Zhang, Zhang, and Zhang (2024), Masini (2025), and Chen and Feng (2023) expose the live frontier: distributional SC has quantile-function tools and mean SC/IFE has factor-model tools, but the finite-donor rank-invariant identification boundary is not characterized.

banked_reason: D0 re-test (post D0-solver upgrade) on prior kernel_substituted parent: First post-restore D0.5 on the original angle 0 v5 proposal returned REVISE@flagship - Theorem 1 (Sharp factor-law identified set) correctness, Section 7 (Estimand-defining functional) correctness. New D0 still produced a derivation whose headline sharpness theorem has correctness gaps relative to the proposal kernel. Pipeline then silently pivoted (rewound_from_stage0_5_pivot) to angle 1 then angle 2; subsequent D0.5 reviews on the pivoted angles produced Case 6b reject (Conjecture 1 refuted by note own finite-support counterexample, Conjecture 2 flagship monotone-selection not proved, Theorem 1 collapses to definition restatement). Run stopped mid-angle-2 pivot per user direction. Apples-to-apples verdict on original kernel: new D0 did not resolve headline-sharpness correctness shortfall; classification remains kernel_substituted.
